does: This paper presents CapSeal, a security architecture that mediates access to API credentials and SSH secrets for AI agents through a trusted local broker rather than exposing secrets directly. The system enforces constrained invocations using capability issuance, schema-validated HTTP execution, broker-controlled SSH actions, anti-replay session binding, and tamper-evident audit logging. The authors implement a Rust prototype integrated with MCP (Model Context Protocol) adapters and formulate security goals around non-disclosure, constrained use, replay resistance, and auditability. They define an evaluation plan to test resilience against prompt injection attacks, tool misuse exploits, and SSH credential abuse scenarios, fundamentally shifting the trust model so agents cannot both access and exfiltrate the same secrets.
connects: This work addresses critical vulnerabilities in current agentic AI deployments that inherit credential handling patterns from traditional system administration—environment variables and forwarded sockets—designed before autonomous language models could execute arbitrary tool calls. It builds on capability-based security principles from operating systems and distributed systems, applying them to the modern problem of agent-mediated API access. The work responds to emerging concerns around prompt injection and tool misuse that have motivated prior research into constraining model behavior and tool use, while drawing on established techniques like session binding, audit logging, and schema validation used in secure service architectures. It extends threat modeling for LLM systems by focusing specifically on the secret-handling attack surface that prior work on agent safety and API security had not fully addressed.
enables: CapSeal establishes a foundation for building provably safer agentic AI systems by separating the capability to invoke external services from the ability to access their credentials. This capability-based approach could enable stronger security properties verifiable in deployed systems, supporting broader adoption of AI agents in security-sensitive domains like infrastructure management, cloud operations, and financial services. The work points toward standardized secret mediation protocols across AI deployment frameworks and suggests how capability systems might generalize beyond credential handling to other sensitive operations. It enables future research into formal verification of agent-secret interactions, runtime enforcement of fine-grained access policies, and secure credential rotation in agentic contexts. The architecture also supports designing agents with genuinely sandboxed permissions where security properties can be maintained even against adversarial prompt injection or model-controlled exploitation.
